1:
"Am I not an apostle? am I not free? have I not seen Jesus Christ our Lord? are not ye my work in the Lord?" The witnesses note Paul's assertion of his apostleship and freedom. Chrysostom notes that, having said he would eat no flesh forever if it made his brother stumble, Paul now shows by deeds something he had slighted — proving by his own example the self-denial he urged, lest any think his words mere boasting. The witnesses note Paul asserting, by a series of questions, that he is an apostle, free, has seen the Lord, and that the Corinthians are his work in the Lord; the establishing of his apostolic standing and rights — his apostleship (seeing the risen Christ, founding the Corinthian church), his liberty (free as any), the ground for the argument that he had rights which he yet forwent for the gospel; the apostle's credentials. The witnesses note am I not an apostle? The witnesses note have I not seen Jesus Christ?

The witnesses note Paul asserting his apostleship, freedom, sight of the risen Christ, and the Corinthians as his work — establishing his rights, which he yet forwent.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's assertion of his apostleship and freedom. The lesson the expositors draw is the establishing of his apostolic standing and rights, the ground for the argument that he forwent them for the gospel. The witnesses note Chrysostom's note on the connection: Chrysostom notes Paul, having said he would forgo flesh forever, now shows by deeds his self-denial — proving by example what he urged; the connection; the example; the deeds; the self-denial proved; the not mere boasting. The expositors observe the apostleship: "Am I not an apostle? am I not free?" — Paul asserts he is an apostle and free; the apostleship; the freedom; the apostle; the liberty; the standing. The witnesses press the sight of Christ: "have I not seen Jesus Christ our Lord?" — Paul has seen the risen Christ (on the Damascus road, and in visions) — a mark of apostleship; the sight of Christ; the seen the Lord; the apostolic credential; the risen Christ; the witness. The expositors draw the lesson on the apostolic credentials: Paul's credentials — an apostle, free, having seen the risen Lord, having founded the Corinthian church; the credentials; the apostleship established; the marks; the standing; the qualifications. The witnesses observe the Corinthians as his work: "are not ye my work in the Lord?" — the Corinthians are Paul's work in the Lord — the church he founded, the proof of his apostleship; the work; the church founded; the proof; the seal; the fruit. The expositors note the ground for the argument: by establishing his apostleship and rights, Paul lays the ground for the argument that he had rights (to maintenance, etc.) which he yet forwent for the gospel; the ground; the rights established; the argument; the forgoing; the self-denial. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the am I not an apostle, the have I not seen Jesus Christ, and the apostle's credentials. The verse thus records Paul's assertion of his apostleship and freedom — teaching the establishing of his standing and rights, the ground for the argument that he forwent them for the gospel.
2:
"If I be not an apostle unto others, yet doubtless I am to you: for the seal of mine apostleship are ye in the Lord." The witnesses note the Corinthians as the seal of Paul's apostleship. Gill notes this is said by way of supposition, not concession — for Paul was an apostle to many; but be that as it may, he was certainly an apostle to the Corinthians. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, even if he were not an apostle to others, he certainly was to the Corinthians, for they are the seal of his apostleship in the Lord; the Corinthian church the proof and confirmation of his apostleship — their conversion and existence as a church, wrought through his ministry, the very seal (authenticating mark) that he was sent of God; the seal of the apostleship. The witnesses note an apostle to you. The witnesses note the seal of mine apostleship.

The witnesses note the Corinthians as the seal of Paul's apostleship — their conversion the authenticating proof that he was sent of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the Corinthians as the seal of the apostleship. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Corinthian church is the proof and confirmation of Paul's apostleship. The witnesses note the supposition: "if I be not an apostle unto others, yet doubtless I am to you" — Gill notes this is supposition, not concession (Paul was an apostle to many); but he was certainly an apostle to the Corinthians; the supposition; the to you; the certainly; the apostle to them; the standing. The expositors observe the seal: "the seal of mine apostleship are ye in the Lord" — the Corinthians are the seal of Paul's apostleship; the seal; the proof; the confirmation; the authenticating mark; the evidence. The witnesses press the meaning of the seal: a seal authenticates, confirms, proves — the Corinthian church, converted through Paul's ministry, authenticates his apostleship; the seal; the authenticating; the confirmation; the proof; the validation. The expositors draw the lesson on the church as the proof: the existence of the Corinthian church (converted, established through Paul) is the proof that he was sent of God — for God blessed his ministry; the proof; the church the evidence; the blessed ministry; the seal; the confirmation. The witnesses observe the conversion as the seal: their conversion — wrought through his preaching — is the seal; the souls won the proof; the converts the evidence; the fruit the confirmation; the seal of the ministry. The expositors note the ground "in the Lord": the seal is "in the Lord" — the church's existence and conversion are by the Lord's blessing on Paul's ministry; the in the Lord; the by the Lord; the blessing; the grace; the divine work. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the an apostle to you, the seal of mine apostleship, and the church as the proof. The verse thus records the Corinthians as the seal of the apostleship — teaching that the Corinthian church is the proof and confirmation of Paul's apostleship.
3:
"Mine answer to them that do examine me is this," The witnesses note Paul's defence to his examiners. Gill notes these words belong to the preceding — that when any called his apostleship in question and examined him, this (his apostleship and its seal) was his answer in defence of himself. The witnesses note Paul stating that his answer to those who examine (question, scrutinize) him is this — namely, the proof of his apostleship (v.1-2) and the rights it carried; the apostle's defence against his critics — that, against those who questioned his standing, he points to his apostleship, his sight of Christ, and the Corinthian church as his vindication; the answer to the examiners. The witnesses note them that do examine me. The witnesses note mine answer is this.

The witnesses note Paul's defence to his examiners — pointing to his apostleship and its seal as his vindication against the critics.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's defence to his examiners. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostle's vindication against those who questioned his standing, pointing to his apostleship and its seal. The witnesses note the examiners: "them that do examine me" — Gill notes those who called his apostleship in question and examined him; the examiners; the critics; the questioners; the scrutinizers; the accusers. The expositors observe the answer: "mine answer to them that do examine me is this" — Paul's answer to them is this — the proof of his apostleship (v.1-2) and its rights; the answer; the defence; the reply; the vindication; the response. The witnesses press the connection: Gill notes these words belong to the preceding — Paul's apostleship and its seal (v.1-2) being his answer; the connection; the answer the apostleship; the defence the seal; the vindication; the proof. The expositors draw the lesson on the apostle's vindication: against those who questioned his standing, Paul points to his apostleship, his sight of Christ, and the Corinthian church; the vindication; the defence; the proof; the answer; the standing. The witnesses observe the critics at Corinth: there were those at Corinth who questioned Paul's apostleship (perhaps the false apostles, or his refusal of maintenance taken as a mark against him); the critics; the questioners; the false apostles; the doubts; the opposition. The expositors note the calm defence: Paul defends himself calmly, by the plain proofs — his apostleship, his work; the calm defence; the plain proofs; the vindication; the answer; the response. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the them that do examine me, the mine answer is this, and the apostle's defence. The verse thus records Paul's defence to his examiners — teaching the apostle's vindication against those who questioned his standing, pointing to his apostleship and its seal.
4:
"Have we not power to eat and to drink?" The witnesses note Paul's right to maintenance. Gill notes that, having proved his apostleship, Paul establishes his right to a maintenance as a gospel minister — by "power to eat and drink" he means not the common right of mankind, but the right to be maintained at the church's expense. The witnesses note Paul asserting his right (power) to eat and drink — that is, to be maintained by the churches he served; the first of the apostle's rights — the right of the gospel minister to a livelihood from those he serves, established as a lawful claim; the right Paul possessed (and would prove at length, v.7-14) yet forwent (v.15); the right to maintenance. The witnesses note power to eat and to drink. The witnesses note the right to maintenance.

The witnesses note Paul's right to be maintained by the churches — the gospel minister's lawful claim to a livelihood, which Paul possessed yet forwent.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's right to maintenance. The lesson the expositors draw is the gospel minister's lawful right to a livelihood from those he serves, which Paul possessed yet forwent. The witnesses note the right: "have we not power to eat and to drink?" — Gill notes Paul means not the common right of mankind, but the right to be maintained at the church's expense; the right; the power; the maintenance; the livelihood; the claim. The expositors observe the maintenance: the "power to eat and drink" is the right to be maintained — to receive food and support from the churches; the maintenance; the support; the livelihood; the provision; the keep. The witnesses press the lawful claim: the right to maintenance is a lawful claim — the gospel minister may rightly receive a livelihood from those he serves; the lawful claim; the right; the just; the proper; the legitimate. The expositors draw the lesson on the minister's livelihood: the gospel minister has a right to a livelihood from those he serves — established here, to be proved at length (v.7-14); the livelihood; the minister's right; the support; the maintenance; the claim. The witnesses observe the right possessed yet forgone: Paul possessed this right (and would prove it) yet forwent it (v.15) — the point of the chapter; the right possessed; the forgone; the self-denial; the not used; the surrendered. The expositors note the first of the rights: this is the first of the apostle's rights (eat and drink, maintenance) — followed by the right to a wife (v.5), to forbear working (v.6); the first; the rights listed; the maintenance; the catalogue; the claims. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the power to eat and to drink, the right to maintenance, and the right possessed yet forgone. The verse thus records Paul's right to maintenance — teaching the gospel minister's lawful right to a livelihood, which Paul possessed yet forwent.
5:
"Have we not power to lead about a sister, a wife, as well as other apostles, and as the brethren of the Lord, and Cephas?" The witnesses note the apostles' right to marry and to support a wife. Gill notes "a sister, a wife" is a Hebraism (a believing wife); the sense the papists put on it (a woman not a wife, to minister) is rejected — the other apostles, the Lord's brethren, and Cephas (Peter) had believing wives whom they led about and were maintained with. The witnesses note Paul asserting his right to lead about a believing wife (a "sister") and have her maintained, as did the other apostles, the Lord's brethren, and Peter; the apostles' right to marriage and to a wife's support — a right exercised by Peter and others (refuting the later notion of apostolic celibacy), which Paul also had though he did not use it; the right to a wife. The witnesses note a sister, a wife. The witnesses note other apostles, and Cephas.

The witnesses note the apostles' right to a believing wife and her support — exercised by Peter and others — refuting apostolic celibacy.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles' right to marry. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostles' right to a believing wife and her support, exercised by Peter and others, refuting the notion of apostolic celibacy. The witnesses note the right: "have we not power to lead about a sister, a wife" — Gill notes "a sister, a wife" is a Hebraism (a believing wife); Paul asserts the right to lead about a believing wife and have her maintained; the right; the wife; the sister; the believing wife; the support. The expositors observe the rejection of the papist gloss: Gill rejects the papist sense (a woman, not a wife, to minister) — the meaning is a believing wife, whom the apostle had the right to marry and to have maintained; the rejection; the not a mere woman; the believing wife; the marriage; the papist gloss refuted. The witnesses press the example of the apostles: "as well as other apostles, and as the brethren of the Lord, and Cephas" — the other apostles, the Lord's brethren, and Cephas (Peter) had believing wives whom they led about; the example; the other apostles; the Lord's brethren; the Cephas; the married apostles. The expositors draw the lesson on the right to marry: the apostles had the right to marry and to a wife's support — a lawful right, exercised by Peter and others; the right to marry; the marriage; the lawful; the apostles married; the wife. The witnesses observe the refuting of apostolic celibacy: the fact that Peter (and others) had wives refutes the later notion of mandatory apostolic/clerical celibacy; the refuting; the Peter married; the celibacy refuted; the apostles' wives; the marriage lawful. The expositors note Paul's not using the right: Paul had this right (to a wife and her support) but did not use it — remaining unmarried for the gospel; the not using; the unmarried; the forgone; the for the gospel; the self-denial. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the a sister, a wife, the other apostles and Cephas, and the right to a wife. The verse thus records the apostles' right to marry — teaching the right to a believing wife and her support, exercised by Peter and others, refuting apostolic celibacy.
6:
"Or I only and Barnabas, have not we power to forbear working?" The witnesses note Paul and Barnabas's right to forbear working. Gill notes Paul and Barnabas were long companions; "are we alone exempted from those rights common to others?" Barnes notes they had wrought together as tentmakers, but this did not mean they had no claim to support. The witnesses note Paul asking whether he and Barnabas alone lacked the right to forbear working (to be supported without labouring at a trade); the right to be free from manual labour — that the apostles, like other gospel ministers, had the right to be maintained and so to give themselves wholly to the ministry, though Paul and Barnabas chose to work; the right to forbear working. The witnesses note I only and Barnabas. The witnesses note power to forbear working.

The witnesses note Paul and Barnabas's right to forbear working — to be supported and give themselves wholly to the ministry, though they chose to labour.

So the witnesses make the verse the right to forbear working. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostles' right to be supported and free from manual labour, though Paul and Barnabas chose to work. The witnesses note the question: "or I only and Barnabas, have not we power to forbear working?" — Gill notes Paul asks whether he and Barnabas alone are exempted from the rights common to others; the question; the I only and Barnabas; the forbear working; the right; the exemption. The expositors observe the right: "power to forbear working" — the right to forbear working (to be supported without labouring at a trade); the right; the forbear working; the not labouring; the maintenance; the support. The witnesses press the right to give wholly to the ministry: the right to forbear working means the right to be maintained and so to give oneself wholly to the ministry; the giving wholly; the ministry; the supported; the free for the work; the devotion. The expositors draw the lesson on the apostles' right: the apostles, like other gospel ministers, had the right to be maintained and free from manual labour; the apostles' right; the maintenance; the free from labour; the support; the right. The witnesses observe Paul and Barnabas's choice: Paul and Barnabas chose to work (as tentmakers, Acts 18:3) — not because they lacked the right, but by choice, for the gospel; the choice; the working; the tentmakers; the by choice; the self-denial. The expositors note Barnes's note: Barnes notes they had wrought as tentmakers, but this did not mean they had no claim to support; the claim; the right; the working not forfeiting the right; the choice; the maintenance due. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I only and Barnabas, the power to forbear working, and the right to give wholly to the ministry. The verse thus records the right to forbear working — teaching the apostles' right to be supported and free from labour, though Paul and Barnabas chose to work.
7:
"Who goeth a warfare any time at his own charges? who planteth a vineyard, and eateth not of the fruit thereof? or who feedeth a flock, and eateth not of the milk of the flock?" The witnesses note the three analogies for the minister's support. Gill notes the soldier serves at the public charge; the apostle argues from common practice that gospel ministers, being soldiers of Christ, should be maintained. The witnesses note Paul arguing for the minister's support by three analogies — the soldier (who serves at another's charges), the vinedresser (who eats the fruit), and the shepherd (who drinks the milk); the natural justice of the minister's maintenance, drawn from common life — as the soldier, the farmer, and the shepherd are supported by their labour, so the gospel minister, labouring in Christ's warfare, vineyard, and flock, should be supported; the three analogies. The witnesses note who goeth a warfare at his own charges? The witnesses note eateth of the fruit, drinketh of the milk.

The witnesses note three analogies for the minister's support — the soldier, the vinedresser, the shepherd — the natural justice of the minister's maintenance.

So the witnesses make the verse the three analogies for the minister's support. The lesson the expositors draw is the natural justice of the minister's maintenance, drawn from common life. The witnesses note the soldier: "who goeth a warfare any time at his own charges?" — Gill notes the soldier serves at the public charge; the soldier; the warfare; the charges; the supported soldier; the analogy. The expositors observe the vinedresser: "who planteth a vineyard, and eateth not of the fruit thereof?" — the vinedresser eats the fruit of his vineyard; the vinedresser; the vineyard; the fruit; the eating; the analogy. The witnesses press the shepherd: "who feedeth a flock, and eateth not of the milk of the flock?" — the shepherd drinks the milk of his flock; the shepherd; the flock; the milk; the drinking; the analogy. The expositors draw the lesson on the natural justice: the three analogies show the natural justice of the minister's maintenance — as the soldier, farmer, and shepherd are supported by their labour, so should the minister; the natural justice; the common life; the labourer supported; the just; the analogy. The witnesses observe the minister's labour: the gospel minister labours in Christ's warfare (the soldier), vineyard (the vinedresser), and flock (the shepherd) — and so should be supported; the labour; the warfare; the vineyard; the flock; the minister's work. The expositors note the argument from common practice: Paul argues from common practice — what all acknowledge in soldier, farmer, shepherd — that the minister should be supported; the argument; the common practice; the acknowledged; the natural; the just. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who goeth a warfare at his own charges, the eateth of the fruit and milk, and the natural justice of maintenance. The verse thus records the three analogies for the minister's support — teaching the natural justice of the minister's maintenance, drawn from common life.
8:
"Say I these things as a man? or saith not the law the same also?" The witnesses note the appeal from common life to the law. Barnes notes Paul asks whether he speaks this on his own authority, or whether this reasonable thing is supported by the authority of God — the law of Moses saying the same. The witnesses note Paul moving from the argument of common life (the soldier, vineyard, flock) to the higher authority of the law, asking whether he speaks merely as a man or whether the law says the same; the grounding of the minister's right not only in natural justice but in the divine law — that what reason and common practice teach, the law of God also confirms; the appeal to the law. The witnesses note say I these things as a man? The witnesses note saith not the law the same?

The witnesses note Paul appealing from common life to the law — the minister's right grounded not only in natural justice but in the divine law.

So the witnesses make the verse the appeal from common life to the law. The lesson the expositors draw is the grounding of the minister's right not only in natural justice but in the divine law. The witnesses note the question: "say I these things as a man?" — Barnes notes Paul asks whether he speaks on his own authority (merely as a man, from common reasoning); the question; the as a man; the own authority; the common reasoning; the human argument. The expositors observe the appeal to the law: "or saith not the law the same also?" — Paul asks whether the law (of Moses) says the same; the appeal; the law; the same; the divine authority; the Scripture. The witnesses press the grounding in the law: the minister's right is grounded not only in natural justice (the soldier, vineyard, flock, v.7) but in the divine law; the grounding; the law; the divine; the higher authority; the Scripture. The expositors draw the lesson on the law confirming reason: what reason and common practice teach, the law of God also confirms — the two agree; the law confirming; the reason and the law; the agreement; the divine seal; the confirmation. The witnesses observe the higher authority: Paul moves to the higher authority — not merely human reasoning, but the law of God; the higher authority; the law; the divine; the Scripture; the authority. The expositors note the strengthening of the argument: the appeal to the law strengthens the argument — the right rests not only on reason but on God's word; the strengthening; the argument; the law; the firmer ground; the divine support. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the say I these things as a man, the saith not the law the same, and the appeal to the law. The verse thus records the appeal from common life to the law — teaching the grounding of the minister's right not only in natural justice but in the divine law.
9:
"For it is written in the law of Moses, Thou shalt not muzzle the mouth of the ox that treadeth out the corn. Doth God take care for oxen?" The witnesses note the law of the unmuzzled ox. Barnes notes the citation from Deuteronomy 25:4 — to muzzle means to bind the mouth to prevent eating; the ox treading out the corn was not to be muzzled, but allowed to eat. The witnesses note Paul citing the law that forbids muzzling the ox that treads the corn, and asking whether God's care is for oxen merely; the principle that the labourer should share in the fruit of his labour — the law, though about the ox, was given chiefly for a higher purpose (v.10), teaching that those who labour (the minister) should be maintained; the ox and the principle. The witnesses note thou shalt not muzzle the ox. The witnesses note doth God take care for oxen?

The witnesses note the law of the unmuzzled ox — the labourer sharing in the fruit of his labour; the law given chiefly for a higher purpose.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of the unmuzzled ox. The lesson the expositors draw is the principle that the labourer should share in the fruit of his labour, the law given chiefly for a higher purpose. The witnesses note the citation: "Thou shalt not muzzle the mouth of the ox that treadeth out the corn" — Barnes notes the citation from Deuteronomy 25:4; the ox treading out the corn was not to be muzzled, but allowed to eat; the citation; the unmuzzled ox; the law; the Deuteronomy; the corn. The expositors observe the practice: the ox, treading the corn (to thresh it), was to be allowed to eat of it — not muzzled; the practice; the threshing; the eating; the not muzzled; the ox fed. The witnesses press the question: "Doth God take care for oxen?" — Paul asks whether God's care is for oxen merely; the question; the for oxen; the higher purpose; the not merely the ox; the deeper meaning. The expositors draw the lesson on the higher purpose: the law, though about the ox, was given chiefly for a higher purpose (v.10) — teaching that those who labour should share in the fruit; the higher purpose; the chiefly for men; the deeper meaning; the principle; the labourer. The witnesses observe the principle of the labourer's share: the principle — the labourer should share in the fruit of his labour (as the ox eats the corn it treads); the principle; the labourer's share; the fruit; the sharing; the just. The expositors note the application to the minister: the principle applies to the minister — who labours in the gospel and should be maintained by it; the application; the minister; the labour; the maintenance; the share. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the thou shalt not muzzle the ox, the doth God take care for oxen, and the labourer's share. The verse thus records the law of the unmuzzled ox — teaching the principle that the labourer should share in the fruit of his labour, the law given chiefly for a higher purpose.
10:
"Or saith he it altogether for our sakes? For our sakes, no doubt, this is written: that he that ploweth should plow in hope; and that he that thresheth in hope should be partaker of his hope." The witnesses note that the law was written chiefly for our sakes. Gill notes God delivered the law about the ox not merely for the ox's sake, but chiefly for the sake of men — the Jewish writers themselves saying the ox is mentioned to express man. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the law (of the unmuzzled ox) was written altogether (chiefly) for our sakes — that the plowman should plow in hope and the thresher partake of his hope; the higher application — the law's deeper purpose being to teach that the labourer (the minister) should work in the hope of sharing in the fruit; the law for our sakes. The witnesses note for our sakes. The witnesses note plow in hope, partaker of his hope.

The witnesses note the law of the ox written chiefly for our sakes — the labourer working in hope of sharing the fruit; the deeper purpose.

So the witnesses make the verse the law written for our sakes. The lesson the expositors draw is the law's deeper purpose, teaching that the labourer should work in the hope of sharing the fruit. The witnesses note the question and answer: "saith he it altogether for our sakes? For our sakes, no doubt, this is written" — Gill notes God delivered the law not merely for the ox, but chiefly for men; the for our sakes; the chiefly for men; the deeper purpose; the no doubt; the higher meaning. The expositors observe the plowman: "he that ploweth should plow in hope" — the plowman plows in hope (of the harvest, of sharing the fruit); the plowman; the plowing; the hope; the harvest; the labour. The witnesses press the thresher: "he that thresheth in hope should be partaker of his hope" — the thresher threshes in hope, and should partake of his hope (share in the fruit); the thresher; the threshing; the hope; the partaking; the share. The expositors draw the lesson on the labourer's hope: the law teaches that the labourer should work in the hope of sharing the fruit — and should indeed partake; the labourer's hope; the working in hope; the sharing; the partaking; the reward. The witnesses observe the application to the minister: the minister, like the plowman and thresher, labours in hope and should share in the fruit (be maintained); the application; the minister; the labour; the hope; the maintenance. The expositors note the deeper purpose of the law: the law's deeper purpose was not the ox merely, but the principle — the labourer's right to share; the deeper purpose; the principle; the higher meaning; the for our sakes; the teaching. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for our sakes, the plow in hope, and the labourer's hope. The verse thus records the law written for our sakes — teaching the law's deeper purpose, that the labourer should work in the hope of sharing the fruit.
11:
"If we have sown unto you spiritual things, is it a great thing if we shall reap your carnal things?" The witnesses note the just exchange of spiritual for temporal. Gill notes the preachers of the gospel are compared to sowers; the seed they sow is the word of God; if they have sown the choice spiritual seed, it is no great thing to reap temporal support. The witnesses note Paul arguing that, having sown spiritual things (the gospel) among the Corinthians, it is no great thing for the ministers to reap their carnal (temporal) things; the equity of the exchange — the minister gives the greater (spiritual, eternal benefits) and receives the lesser (material support), so the maintenance is a small and just return; the just exchange. The witnesses note sown spiritual things. The witnesses note reap your carnal things.

The witnesses note the just exchange — having sown spiritual things, it is no great thing to reap temporal support; the minister gives the greater.

So the witnesses make the verse the just exchange of spiritual for temporal. The lesson the expositors draw is the equity of the exchange — the minister gives the greater spiritual benefits and receives the lesser material support. The witnesses note the sowing: "if we have sown unto you spiritual things" — Gill notes the preachers are sowers, the seed the word of God; the ministers sowed spiritual things (the gospel) among the Corinthians; the sowing; the spiritual things; the gospel; the seed; the word. The expositors observe the reaping: "is it a great thing if we shall reap your carnal things?" — it is no great thing for the ministers to reap the Corinthians' carnal (temporal, material) things; the reaping; the carnal things; the temporal; the material; the support. The witnesses press the equity: the exchange is equitable — the minister gives the greater (spiritual, eternal), receives the lesser (material, temporal); the equity; the greater for the lesser; the just; the fair; the exchange. The expositors draw the lesson on the just return: the maintenance is a small and just return for the great spiritual benefit the minister brings; the just return; the small; the fair; the maintenance; the recompense. The witnesses observe the value of the spiritual over the temporal: the spiritual things sown (eternal benefits) far exceed the carnal things reaped (material support); the value; the spiritual over the temporal; the greater; the eternal; the exceeding. The expositors note the no-great-thing: it is "no great thing" — a small matter — for the minister to receive temporal support for spiritual labour; the no great thing; the small; the slight; the modest; the just return. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the sown spiritual things, the reap your carnal things, and the just exchange. The verse thus records the just exchange of spiritual for temporal — teaching the equity of the exchange, the minister giving the greater and receiving the lesser.
12:
"If others be partakers of this power over you, are not we rather? Nevertheless we have not used this power; but suffer all things, lest we should hinder the gospel of Christ." The witnesses note Paul's forgoing of his right for the gospel. Gill notes "this power" is the right of a maintenance, which others (the true ministers, or even false apostles) justly claimed and enjoyed; if they had this right, Paul had it the more. The witnesses note Paul arguing that, if others have the right of maintenance over the Corinthians, he has it the more (as their father in the faith), yet he has not used this right, but suffers all things lest he hinder the gospel; the supreme example again — Paul forgoing his clear right rather than place any obstacle before the gospel, enduring hardship and self-support that the gospel be not hindered; the right forgone for the gospel. The witnesses note we have not used this power. The witnesses note lest we should hinder the gospel.

The witnesses note Paul forgoing his right of maintenance — suffering all things lest he hinder the gospel; the right forgone for the gospel's sake.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's forgoing of his right for the gospel. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul forwent his clear right rather than place any obstacle before the gospel. The witnesses note the greater right: "if others be partakers of this power over you, are not we rather?" — Gill notes "this power" is the right of maintenance, which others claimed; if they had it, Paul had it the more (as their father in the faith); the greater right; the others; the rather; the more right; the claim. The expositors observe the forgoing: "nevertheless we have not used this power" — yet Paul has not used this right; the forgoing; the not used; the right forgone; the not claimed; the surrendered. The witnesses press the suffering: "but suffer all things" — Paul suffers all things (hardship, self-support, want) rather than use his right; the suffering; the hardship; the enduring; the self-support; the cost. The expositors draw the lesson on the reason — lest he hinder the gospel: "lest we should hinder the gospel of Christ" — Paul forwent his right lest he hinder the gospel — lest his receiving support give occasion for the charge of preaching for gain; the reason; the lest he hinder; the gospel's sake; the no obstacle; the free gospel. The witnesses observe the supreme example: Paul's forgoing his right is the supreme example (again) — giving up a clear right rather than place any obstacle before the gospel; the supreme example; the right forgone; the self-denial; the for the gospel; the sacrifice. The expositors note the gospel unhindered: the aim was the gospel unhindered — that nothing in Paul's conduct give occasion to reject or reproach it; the gospel unhindered; the no obstacle; the free course; the unimpeded; the advanced. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we have not used this power, the lest we should hinder the gospel, and the right forgone. The verse thus records Paul's forgoing of his right for the gospel — teaching that Paul forwent his clear right rather than hinder the gospel, suffering all things for its sake.
13:
"Do ye not know that they which minister about holy things live of the things of the temple? and they which wait at the altar are partakers with the altar?" The witnesses note the example of the priests' maintenance. Chrysostom notes Paul takes great care to show that the receiving was not forbidden — adducing the law expressly given concerning the priests, a closer example than the oxen. The witnesses note Paul citing the example of those who minister about holy things, living of the temple, and those who wait at the altar, sharing with the altar; the further argument for the minister's maintenance — drawn from the priests and Levites under the law, who were supported from the temple offerings and the altar, showing that God himself ordained that those who serve in sacred things be maintained by them; the priests' maintenance. The witnesses note minister about holy things. The witnesses note partakers with the altar.

The witnesses note the priests' maintenance — those who serve at the altar share with it — showing God ordained that sacred ministers be supported.

So the witnesses make the verse the example of the priests' maintenance. The lesson the expositors draw is that God ordained, in the law, that those who serve in sacred things be maintained by them. The witnesses note the ministers of holy things: "they which minister about holy things live of the things of the temple" — those who minister about holy things (the priests, Levites) live of the temple (the offerings, tithes); the ministers; the holy things; the temple; the living; the support. The expositors observe the altar: "they which wait at the altar are partakers with the altar" — those who wait at the altar (the priests) share with the altar (partake of the sacrifices); the altar; the waiting; the partaking; the sharing; the sacrifices. The witnesses press Chrysostom's note on the closer example: Chrysostom notes Paul adduces the law expressly given concerning the priests — a closer example than the oxen; the closer example; the priests; the express law; the sacred ministers; the apt example. The expositors draw the lesson on God's ordinance: God himself ordained, in the law, that those who serve in sacred things be maintained by them — the priests from the temple and altar; the ordinance; the God ordained; the sacred ministers; the maintenance; the law. The witnesses observe the application to the gospel minister: the example applies to the gospel minister — who serves in sacred things and should be maintained; the application; the gospel minister; the sacred service; the maintenance; the support. The expositors note the divine pattern: the divine pattern (the priests maintained from the temple) shows that sacred service warrants maintenance; the pattern; the divine; the priests; the maintenance; the warrant. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the minister about holy things, the partakers with the altar, and the priests' maintenance. The verse thus records the example of the priests' maintenance — teaching that God ordained that those who serve in sacred things be maintained by them.
14:
"Even so hath the Lord ordained that they which preach the gospel should live of the gospel." The witnesses note the Lord's ordinance for the gospel minister's support. Gill notes it is an order and appointment of the Lord Jesus Christ that his ministering servants, who labour in preaching, should be sufficiently provided with a comfortable livelihood. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, even so, the Lord has ordained that those who preach the gospel should live of the gospel; the crowning argument — the express command of Christ himself, that gospel ministers be maintained by those they serve (as the priests by the temple); the divine ordinance, resting the minister's right on the Lord's own appointment; the Lord's ordinance. The witnesses note the Lord ordained. The witnesses note live of the gospel.

The witnesses note the Lord's ordinance — those who preach the gospel should live of the gospel — the minister's right resting on Christ's own command.

So the witnesses make the verse the Lord's ordinance for the minister's support. The lesson the expositors draw is the crowning argument — Christ's own command that gospel ministers be maintained. The witnesses note the ordinance: "the Lord hath ordained" — Gill notes it is an order and appointment of the Lord Jesus Christ; the Lord ordained; the appointment; the command; the ordinance; the Christ's order. The expositors observe the substance: "they which preach the gospel should live of the gospel" — those who preach the gospel should live of the gospel (be maintained by it); the substance; the live of the gospel; the maintenance; the support; the minister's livelihood. The witnesses press the crowning argument: this is the crowning argument — the express command of Christ himself (Luke 10:7; Matthew 10:10), that gospel ministers be maintained; the crowning argument; the Christ's command; the express; the divine; the supreme. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine ordinance: the minister's right rests on the Lord's own appointment — not merely reason, the law, or the priests' example, but Christ's express ordinance; the divine ordinance; the Lord's appointment; the express command; the firmest ground; the Christ's order. The witnesses observe the parallel with the priests: as the priests lived of the temple (v.13), so the gospel ministers should live of the gospel; the parallel; the priests and the ministers; the temple and the gospel; the maintenance; the pattern. The expositors note the firm ground of the right: the minister's right rests on the firmest ground — the command of Christ; the firm ground; the Christ's command; the divine; the sure; the established. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Lord ordained, the live of the gospel, and the Lord's ordinance. The verse thus records the Lord's ordinance for the minister's support — teaching the crowning argument, Christ's own command that gospel ministers be maintained.
15:
"But I have used none of these things: neither have I written these things, that it should be so done unto me: for it were better for me to die, than that any man should make my glorying void." The witnesses note Paul's refusal to use his right. Gill notes Paul had used none of these arguments or rights for himself; nor did he write these things that it should be done to him — but it were better to die than to have his glorying (in preaching freely) made void. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he has used none of these rights, nor written to claim them, and that he would rather die than have his glorying made void; the strength of his resolve — that, having established his full right to maintenance, he yet refuses it, and writes not to claim it, holding his free preaching so dear that he would rather die than lose this ground of glorying; the refusal of the right. The witnesses note I have used none of these things. The witnesses note better to die than... my glorying void.

The witnesses note Paul's refusal to use his right — he would rather die than have his glorying in free preaching made void; the strength of his resolve.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's refusal to use his right. The lesson the expositors draw is the strength of his resolve, holding his free preaching so dear that he would rather die than lose this ground of glorying. The witnesses note the refusal: "I have used none of these things" — Gill notes Paul had used none of these arguments or rights for himself; the refusal; the none used; the not claimed; the not employed; the forgone. The expositors observe the not writing to claim: "neither have I written these things, that it should be so done unto me" — Paul did not write these things to claim the right (lest they think he was hinting for support); the not writing to claim; the no hint; the not for himself; the no demand; the disclaimer. The witnesses press the strength of resolve: "it were better for me to die, than that any man should make my glorying void" — Paul would rather die than have his glorying (in free preaching) made void; the strength; the better to die; the glorying; the resolve; the determination. The expositors draw the lesson on his glorying: Paul's glorying was in preaching the gospel free of charge — a ground of joy and rejoicing he would not lose; the glorying; the free preaching; the joy; the rejoicing; the ground. The witnesses observe the dearness of the free preaching: Paul held his free preaching so dear that he would rather die than lose it — the dearness; the precious; the free preaching; the cherished; the held fast. The expositors note the disclaimer of self-interest: Paul disclaims self-interest — he writes not to get support, but to defend his apostleship and his liberty; the disclaimer; the no self-interest; the not for gain; the defence; the pure motive. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I have used none of these things, the better to die than my glorying void, and the refusal of the right. The verse thus records Paul's refusal to use his right — teaching the strength of his resolve, holding his free preaching so dear that he would rather die than lose it.
16:
"For though I preach the gospel, I have nothing to glory of: for necessity is laid upon me; yea, woe is unto me, if I preach not the gospel!" The witnesses note the necessity laid on Paul to preach. Gill notes Paul's sense is that, though he preached the gospel ever so well or freely, he had nothing to glory of in the mere preaching, since necessity was laid upon him. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, though he preaches the gospel, he has nothing to glory of in that, for necessity is laid upon him, and woe to him if he preach not; the constraint of the divine commission — that the bare preaching is no ground of boasting, since Paul preaches under a necessity (the call and command of God), with a woe upon him if he fail; the necessity to preach. The witnesses note necessity is laid upon me. The witnesses note woe is unto me if I preach not.

The witnesses note the necessity laid on Paul to preach — the bare preaching no ground of glorying, since he preaches under divine constraint, with woe if he fail.

So the witnesses make the verse the necessity laid on Paul to preach. The lesson the expositors draw is that the bare preaching is no ground of boasting, since Paul preaches under a divine necessity, with a woe upon him if he fail. The witnesses note the no glorying in preaching: "though I preach the gospel, I have nothing to glory of" — Gill notes that, though Paul preached ever so well or freely, he had nothing to glory of in the mere preaching; the no glorying; the nothing to boast; the preaching; the no ground; the the bare preaching. The expositors observe the necessity: "for necessity is laid upon me" — necessity is laid upon Paul (the call and command of God); the necessity; the constraint; the laid upon; the compulsion; the divine call. The witnesses press the woe: "yea, woe is unto me, if I preach not the gospel!" — woe to Paul if he preach not; the woe; the if he fail; the judgment; the compulsion; the dread. The expositors draw the lesson on the constraint of the commission: Paul preaches under the constraint of the divine commission — not as a free choice he might forgo, but as a necessity laid on him by God; the constraint; the commission; the necessity; the divine call; the compulsion. The witnesses observe the no boast in the necessary: since Paul preaches under necessity (he must, or woe), the bare preaching is no ground of boasting — for he does but what he is bound to do; the no boast; the necessary; the bound; the duty; the no merit. The expositors note the ground of glorying elsewhere: Paul's glorying (v.15) was not in the bare preaching (which he must do), but in preaching free of charge (which he need not do) — the voluntary self-denial his ground; the ground elsewhere; the free preaching; the voluntary; the self-denial; the glorying. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the necessity is laid upon me, the woe is unto me if I preach not, and the necessity to preach. The verse thus records the necessity laid on Paul to preach — teaching that the bare preaching is no ground of boasting, since Paul preaches under divine constraint, with woe if he fail.
17:
"For if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward: but if against my will, a dispensation of the gospel is committed unto me." The witnesses note the willing and the constrained service. Gill notes Paul supposes a case — if he did this thing willingly (freely, without reward, in love), he has a reward; but a dispensation (a stewardship) is committed to him regardless. The witnesses note Paul distinguishing — if he does this willingly (going beyond the bare duty, preaching freely), he has a reward; but if merely under constraint (doing only what he must), a dispensation of the gospel is yet committed to him; the difference between the willing service that earns a reward and the mere discharge of the committed stewardship — the willing, free self-denial being Paul's ground of reward; the willing and the constrained. The witnesses note if I do this willingly. The witnesses note a dispensation committed unto me.

The witnesses note the willing service that has a reward versus the mere discharge of the committed stewardship — the willing self-denial Paul's ground of reward.

So the witnesses make the verse the willing and the constrained service. The lesson the expositors draw is the difference between the willing service that earns a reward and the mere discharge of the committed stewardship. The witnesses note the willing: "if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward" — Gill notes if Paul did this willingly (freely, without reward, in love, going beyond the bare duty), he has a reward; the willing; the freely; the reward; the going beyond; the voluntary. The expositors observe the constrained: "but if against my will, a dispensation of the gospel is committed unto me" — if merely under constraint (doing only what he must), a dispensation (stewardship) is yet committed to him; the constrained; the against the will; the dispensation; the stewardship; the committed. The witnesses press the difference: the difference — the willing service (going beyond, preaching freely) earns a reward; the mere discharge of the committed stewardship (doing only the bound duty) does not; the difference; the willing and the bound; the reward and the no reward; the voluntary and the constrained; the distinction. The expositors draw the lesson on the willing self-denial: Paul's willing self-denial (preaching free of charge, going beyond the bare duty) is his ground of reward; the willing self-denial; the going beyond; the free preaching; the reward; the ground. The witnesses observe the committed dispensation: the "dispensation of the gospel" is the stewardship committed to Paul — which he must discharge (the necessity, v.16); the dispensation; the stewardship; the committed; the must discharge; the trust. The expositors note the reward of the voluntary: the reward attaches to the voluntary — the free, self-denying service beyond the bare duty; the reward; the voluntary; the free; the beyond duty; the self-denial. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the if I do this willingly, the dispensation committed unto me, and the willing self-denial. The verse thus records the willing and the constrained service — teaching the difference between the willing service that earns a reward and the mere discharge of the committed stewardship.
18:
"What is my reward then? Verily that, when I preach the gospel, I may make the gospel of Christ without charge, that I abuse not my power in the gospel." The witnesses note Paul's reward — preaching free of charge. Gill notes Paul has no reward to claim as a debt (he is but an unprofitable servant doing his duty); the only reward that remains is this — to make the gospel without charge. The witnesses note Paul declaring that his reward is this — that, preaching the gospel, he may make it without charge, not abusing his right in the gospel; the reward Paul sought — not money, honour, or even a debt-reward, but the joy of preaching the gospel freely, forgoing his right rather than abusing it; the reward of free preaching. The witnesses note make the gospel without charge. The witnesses note abuse not my power.

The witnesses note Paul's reward — to make the gospel without charge, not abusing his right — the joy of preaching freely his recompense.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's reward of free preaching. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul's reward was the joy of preaching the gospel freely, forgoing his right rather than abusing it. The witnesses note the question: "what is my reward then?" — Gill notes Paul has no reward to claim as a debt; the question; the reward; the what; the recompense; the inquiry. The expositors observe the answer: "that, when I preach the gospel, I may make the gospel of Christ without charge" — Paul's reward is to make the gospel without charge (free of cost to the hearers); the answer; the without charge; the free; the no cost; the gratis. The witnesses press the reward sought: the reward Paul sought was not money, honour, or a debt-reward, but the joy of preaching freely; the reward sought; the not money; the not honour; the joy; the free preaching. The expositors draw the lesson on the not abusing the right: "that I abuse not my power in the gospel" — Paul forwent his right (to support) rather than abuse it (use it in a way that might hinder the gospel or be misread as greed); the not abusing; the right forgone; the no abuse; the careful use; the self-restraint. The witnesses observe the joy of free preaching: Paul's recompense was the joy of preaching the gospel freely — a self-rewarding service, the satisfaction of giving the gospel without cost; the joy; the free preaching; the satisfaction; the self-reward; the recompense. The expositors note the high motive: Paul's motive was high — not gain, but the gospel's free course and his own clear conscience; the high motive; the not gain; the gospel; the conscience; the pure aim. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the make the gospel without charge, the abuse not my power, and the reward of free preaching. The verse thus records Paul's reward of free preaching — teaching that his reward was the joy of preaching the gospel freely, forgoing his right rather than abusing it.
